title: Account Management — Orphan and Stale Privileged Accounts
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the NIST 800-53 programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with NIST 800-53 control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST 800-53 AC-2 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 AC-2 (Account management) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.16: Account Management — Orphan and Stale Privileged Accounts.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.16' running on WinEventLog:Security (4720/4726), ms:o365:management sign-in logs, AD export via scripted input, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Account Management — Orphan and Stale Privileged Accounts' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper NIST 800-53 control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the NIST 800-53 control objective.

dataSources: `WinEventLog:Security` (4720/4726), `o365:management:activity` sign-in logs, AD export via scripted input
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742)
spl:
index=sec sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode IN (4720,4726,4738) earliest=-90d
| stats latest(_time) as last_change by Account_Name
| outputlookup ad_account_last_change.csv
description: Flags accounts not seen in authentication logs within policy windows for AC-2 account management lifecycle reviews.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST 800-53 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes sec and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Pair with `inputlookup` of all privileged SAMAccountNames; (2) compare `last_change` to HR termination dates; (3) alert stale admin accounts; (4) feed IAM remediation queue. Indexes required: `index=sec`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`.
visualization: Table (stale accounts), Time chart (creations/modifications), Single value (orphans). Key fields: `Account_Name`, `last_change`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Authentication data model (Authentication.Authentication).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised security assessment activities** — Planned security control assessments, continuous monitoring scans, and Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) validation tests generate expected control deviation signals that are part of the Assessment & Authorisation process.

2. **System authorisation boundary changes** — During ATO re-authorisation, system boundary expansions, or inheritance model updates, temporary control gaps may appear in the monitoring baseline until the updated security plan is approved by the Authorising Official.

3. **Interconnection Security Agreement (ISA) activity** — Data exchanges with authorised external systems operating under signed ISAs produce cross-boundary traffic that triggers network monitoring alerts but is covered by the formal interconnection agreement.

4. **FIPS 199 impact-level re-categorisation** — When system categorisation changes (e.g., Moderate to High), the transition period produces control baseline mismatches until the enhanced controls are fully implemented per the approved implementation schedule.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nist_800_53_exception_filter` KV Store lookup containing approved assessment schedule entries, POA&M IDs, ISA identifiers, and system re-categorisation project IDs. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches with valid exception entries.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `sec` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `AC-2`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIST 800-53 AC-2 (Account management) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.16: Account Management — Orphan and Stale Privileged Accounts." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742), landing events in `sec` under `WinEventLog:Security`. Security channel alone produces 2000-8000 events per domain-joined host per day depending on audit policy (~2-8 GB/host/month). Domain controllers generate 10x more.

Configure the input through Splunk_TA_windows/local/inputs.conf on each Universal Forwarder. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[WinEventLog://Security]
disabled = 0
index = windows
renderXml = true
current_only = 0
evt_resolve_ad_obj = 1
checkpointInterval = 5
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `EventCode, Account_Name, Account_Domain, Logon_Type, Source_Network_Address, Workstation_Name, Process_Name, TargetUserName, Subject_Account_Name`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=sec earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Pair with `inputlookup` of all privileged SAMAccountNames. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** compare `last_change` to HR termination dates. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert stale admin accounts. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** feed IAM remediation queue. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `latest()` across the last 90 days, grouped by `Account_Name`. For "Account Management — Orphan and Stale Privileged Accounts," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIST 800-53 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. `earliest()` and `latest()` extract the first and last value per group — typically used to measure durations ('first seen' to 'last seen') or to pick the freshest attribute value when multiple events update the same entity.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `Account_Name` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(Account_Name) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.14.16: Account Management — Orphan and Stale Privileged Accounts]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.14.16,reg=NIST_800-53
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=sec sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `Account_Name`, `last_change`. Run:

```spl
index=sec sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table Account_Name, last_change
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.14.16: NIST 800-53 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`stale accounts`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`creations`, `modifications`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`orphans`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'NIST 800-53 current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIST 800-53-specific failure modes:**

• Control baseline selection: NIST 800-53 has Low/Moderate/High/Privacy baselines via FIPS 199 categorisation. A detection written to a High baseline threshold produces false alarms against Low-categorised systems. Join with the system categorisation register `nist_800_53_system_categorization.csv` before thresholding.

• CA-7 continuous monitoring cadence: specifies 'ongoing' authorisation activities. Detections supporting CA-7 must produce metrics at the organisation's ConMon cadence (monthly is common for most agencies) — weekly fires are fine but not themselves evidence of monthly compliance.

• Control inheritance: in a cloud-hybrid deployment, some controls are inherited from the Cloud Service Provider. Detections must not flag 'missing' controls the CSP provides (documented in the SSP via Control Inheritance matrix); reference the SSP appendix and suppress matching findings.

• SP 800-53 Rev 5 restructure: controls were renumbered between Rev 4 and Rev 5. Detections that cite Rev 4 numbering (e.g. AC-2.12) may not match the SSP if the organisation has migrated. Store both Rev numbers in the UC metadata and reconcile.
grandmaExplanation: We flags accounts not seen in authentication logs within policy windows for AC-2 account management lifecycle reviews. That helps federal and agency security reviews match what the systems actually do day to day.
